title: Original Sin vs Personal Responsibility
subtitle: Are we born guilty, or born pure? What the Bible and the Quran each say about Adam's sin and ours
summary: Western Christianity teaches that every human being is born affected by Adam's first sin — a condition called original sin — and that this is why everyone needs the saving work of Christ. Islam teaches a different starting point: every child is born pure, in a natural state called the fitrah; Adam sinned, repented, and was completely forgiven; and no soul carries the guilt of another. This article presents each view from its own scriptures — Romans 5 and Psalm 51 on the Christian side, the Quran's verses on Adam and personal responsibility on the Islamic side — and then compares them, including the Bible's own words in Ezekiel 18 and Deuteronomy 24.

seo.metaTitle: Original Sin vs Personal Responsibility: Islam and Christianity
seo.metaDescription: Does the Bible teach inherited guilt from Adam? Does the Quran? Compare Romans 5 and Psalm 51 with Ezekiel 18, Deuteronomy 24, and the Quran's teaching that every soul is born pure and answers only for itself.
keywords: Adam and Eve in the Quran

sections.title: In One Minute
sections.body:
Original sin is the Western Christian teaching that the sin of Adam, the first human, left every one of his descendants born into a damaged condition — and, in the classic formulation, sharing in his guilt. On this view, no one starts life with a clean record, which is why humanity needs a rescuer: Christ. Islam begins somewhere else entirely. The Quran teaches that every child is born upon the fitrah, a pure natural disposition inclined toward God; that Adam sinned, repented, and was fully forgiven on the spot; and that no soul ever bears the sin of another.

This article lets each tradition make its case from its own scripture. You will read the two Bible passages most often cited for original sin, Romans 5:12 and Psalm 51:5, exactly as they appear in the Bible, and the Quranic verses on Adam and personal accountability exactly as they appear in the Quran. Then the two frameworks are compared — including through the Bible's own striking statement in Ezekiel 18 that a child does not bear the sin of a parent.

sections.title: Why This Question Changes Everything
sections.body:
Few doctrinal questions carry as much weight as this one. What a religion believes about a newborn baby quietly determines its whole map of salvation. If a baby arrives already implicated in an ancient sin, then salvation must mean rescue from a condition no human effort can undo — and in Christianity, that rescue is the death and resurrection of Jesus. If a baby arrives pure, then salvation means something different: staying close to God, and returning to Him in repentance whenever we fail.

Two terms will appear throughout, so let us define them now. Original sin, in Western Christian usage, is not a sin a person commits; it is a state or condition inherited from Adam — a wounded human nature, and in the influential formulation of Augustine of Hippo, a share in Adam's guilt itself. The fitrah, in Islamic usage, is the opposite claim: the innate, uncorrupted disposition toward God and moral truth upon which every human being is created.

Both traditions tell the story of Adam and Eve, the first human couple, and their disobedience in the Garden. The stories begin alike. They end very differently — and the ending is where the two theologies part ways.

sections.title: The Question on the Table
sections.body:
The question is not whether human beings sin. Both Christianity and Islam agree, emphatically, that we do — constantly. The question is what we are born with before we have done anything at all. Did Adam's sin leave his descendants born guilty, or born spiritually damaged, in a way that requires a divine remedy beyond their own repentance? Or does every soul begin with a clean record, accountable only for what it will itself choose?

That single question drags several others behind it. Is it just for guilt to be inherited by people who did not commit the act? What happens to infants who die? Why does everyone die, if death entered the world as a punishment? And what, exactly, did Jesus come to do? Each tradition's answers hang together as a system, which is why this comparison is best done slowly and from the primary texts.

sections.title: How the Doctrine Took Shape
sections.body:
The raw material for the doctrine of original sin is the account in Genesis 3, where Adam and Eve eat from the forbidden tree and are expelled from the Garden. Genesis itself, however, never uses the phrase 'original sin,' and Jewish tradition — which reveres the same account — never developed such a doctrine. The doctrine's classic form was worked out centuries after Jesus, above all by Augustine of Hippo (354–430), the North African bishop whose thought shaped Western Christianity more than any other early figure.

Augustine developed and defended the doctrine during his long controversy with Pelagius, a British ascetic whose followers denied that humans inherit sin from Adam's fall. Augustine argued that humanity sinned 'in Adam' and inherits both a corrupted nature and a real share in guilt. His side prevailed: Pelagianism was condemned as a heresy in 418, and Augustine's position was codified by church councils at Carthage (418) and Orange (529). From there it passed into the mainstream of both Catholic and Protestant theology. The sixteenth-century Protestant Reformers retained the doctrine and, in some traditions, described the fallen human condition in even starker terms.

One important nuance: Eastern Orthodox Christianity never fully adopted Augustine's framework. Orthodox theology speaks of 'ancestral sin' — humanity inherits the consequences of Adam's fall, above all mortality and a weakened nature, but not Adam's personal guilt. So 'original sin' as inherited guilt is specifically a Western Christian development, not a belief of all Christians everywhere.

The Quran, proclaimed in the seventh century, retells the story of Adam with a strikingly different ending — repentance sought, forgiveness granted — and lays down a general principle of personal responsibility that leaves no room for inherited sin. The historical details above (Augustine's dates, the condemnation of 418, and the councils of Carthage and Orange) are drawn from the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy's entries on Augustine and on sin in Christian thought.

sections.title: What the Quran Says
sections.body:
The Quran's starting point for human nature is a word that appears in Surah Ar-Room: the fitrah, the pure natural disposition upon which God creates every person.

فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًۭا ۚ فِطْرَتَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى فَطَرَ ٱلنَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ

'So direct your face toward the religion, inclining to truth. [Adhere to] the fitrah of Allah upon which He has created [all] people. No change should there be in the creation of Allah. That is the correct religion, but most of the people do not know.' (Quran 30:30)

A well-known saying of the Prophet Muhammad, recorded in Sahih al-Bukhari 1385 and Sahih Muslim 2658e, teaches that every child is born upon this fitrah. A newborn, in Islam, is not a guilty creature awaiting cleansing; it is a clean slate already inclined toward its Creator.

What about Adam? The Quran does not minimize his sin. It states it bluntly:

فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ وَعَصَىٰٓ ءَادَمُ رَبَّهُۥ فَغَوَىٰ

'And Adam and his wife ate of it, and their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred.' (Quran 20:121)

But the very next verse transforms how the story is remembered:

ثُمَّ ٱجْتَبَٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ

'Then his Lord chose him and turned to him in forgiveness and guided [him].' (Quran 20:122)

Surah Al-Baqara tells the same ending and names the divine attributes behind it:

فَتَلَقَّىٰٓ ءَادَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ كَلِمَٰتٍۢ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ

'Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful.' (Quran 2:37)

In the Quran, Adam's story closes with forgiveness, not with a wound passed down to his children. And the Quran states the general principle in the plainest possible terms:

مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًۭا

'Whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul. And whoever errs only errs against it. And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And never would We punish until We sent a messenger.' (Quran 17:15)

The same principle is repeated in Surah Al-An'aam:

قُلْ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِى رَبًّۭا وَهُوَ رَبُّ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ ۚ وَلَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ إِلَّا عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُم مَّرْجِعُكُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ

'Say, "Is it other than Allah I should desire as a lord while He is the Lord of all things? And every soul earns not [blame] except against itself, and no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. Then to your Lord is your return, and He will inform you concerning that over which you used to differ."' (Quran 6:164)

Finally, the closing verse of Surah Al-Baqara ties responsibility to capacity — no one is held to account for what lies beyond their power, let alone for an act committed before they existed:

لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۚ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا ٱكْتَسَبَتْ ۗ رَبَّنَا لَا تُؤَاخِذْنَآ إِن نَّسِينَآ أَوْ أَخْطَأْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنَآ إِصْرًۭا كَمَا حَمَلْتَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِۦ ۖ وَٱعْفُ عَنَّا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَآ ۚ أَنتَ مَوْلَىٰنَا فَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَٰفِرِينَ

'Allah does not charge a soul except [with that within] its capacity. It will have [the consequence of] what [good] it has gained, and it will bear [the consequence of] what [evil] it has earned. "Our Lord, do not impose blame upon us if we have forgotten or erred. Our Lord, and lay not upon us a burden like that which You laid upon those before us. Our Lord, and burden us not with that which we have no ability to bear. And pardon us; and forgive us; and have mercy upon us. You are our protector, so give us victory over the disbelieving people."' (Quran 2:286)

Taken together, these verses give Islam's answer in full: born pure, judged individually, forgiven directly. The fall of Adam is a lesson in human weakness and divine mercy — not a hereditary wound.

sections.title: What the Bible Says
sections.body:
Fairness requires hearing the Christian case at its strongest. The doctrine of original sin rests chiefly on the apostle Paul's letter to the Romans:

'Therefore as sin entered into the world through one man, and death through sin; and so death passed to all men, because all sinned.' (Romans 5:12)

In mainstream Western Christian reading, Adam here acts as the representative head of the human race: his one act let sin and death into the world, and the surrounding passage goes on to draw a deliberate parallel between Adam, through whom came condemnation, and Christ, through whom comes justification. Historians of doctrine often note that Augustine read this verse in Latin, where the final phrase was rendered 'in whom all sinned' — that is, all humanity sinned in Adam — whereas modern translations of the Greek, including the one quoted above, read 'because all sinned.' Christian scholars continue to discuss how much weight that difference carries; the doctrine itself does not rest on this verse alone.

The second key text is David's psalm of repentance:

'Behold, I was born in iniquity. In sin my mother conceived me.' (Psalm 51:5)

The classic Christian reading takes this as a confession that sinfulness reaches back to conception itself. Others, including many modern commentators, read it as the poetry of a broken man expressing how completely sin pervades his life — a cry of anguish rather than a doctrinal statement about all newborns. Both readings exist within Christian scholarship.

Yet the Bible also contains passages that speak with remarkable directness against the transfer of sin from one person to another. The prophet Ezekiel devotes an entire chapter to rejecting a proverb which claimed that children suffer for their fathers' sins:

'The soul who sins, he shall die. The son shall not bear the iniquity of the father, neither shall the father bear the iniquity of the son. The righteousness of the righteous shall be on him, and the wickedness of the wicked shall be on him.' (Ezekiel 18:20)

The Law of Moses states the same principle as a rule of justice:

'The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers. Every man shall be put to death for his own sin.' (Deuteronomy 24:16)

Christian interpreters generally respond that these passages concern individual judgment and human courts within Israel's covenant, while Romans 5 speaks of humanity's standing before God on a different level. That response deserves to be stated fairly — and weighed against the plain wording of the texts.

Finally, there are the words of Jesus himself about children:

'Most certainly I tell you, unless you turn, and become as little children, you will in no way enter into the Kingdom of Heaven.' (Matthew 18:3)

Christian commentators usually read this as a lesson in humility and trust. Muslims notice something further: Jesus holds up the child — not the baptized adult — as the model of one fit for the Kingdom, which sits naturally with the Islamic teaching that children are born pure.

sections.title: Reading the Texts Side by Side
sections.body:
Set the quoted texts next to each other and the pattern is hard to miss. Ezekiel 18:20 says the son 'shall not bear the iniquity of the father.' Deuteronomy 24:16 says every man answers 'for his own sin.' The Quran says 'no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (17:15, 6:164) and 'every soul earns not [blame] except against itself' (6:164). On the principle of individual responsibility, the Hebrew prophets and the Quran speak with one voice.

The tension appears when Romans 5:12 is added. Even there, the verse as translated from the Greek ends with personal agency — death passed to all 'because all sinned.' The strong Augustinian reading, in which all humanity shares Adam's very guilt, leaned on a Latin rendering that modern Christian translations have set aside. What remains debated within Christianity is how much of the doctrine survives that correction: most Western churches still teach an inherited fallen condition, while Eastern Orthodoxy confines the inheritance to mortality and weakness, not guilt.

The deepest difference, though, is not a single verse but the ending of Adam's story. In the Augustinian telling, the Garden closes with an unhealed wound that only the cross, centuries later, can treat. In the Quran's telling, the wound is healed before Adam leaves the story: 'Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance' (Quran 2:37); 'Then his Lord chose him and turned to him in forgiveness and guided [him]' (Quran 20:122). Where one tradition sees a debt accumulating across millennia, the other sees a door — repentance — that has stood open since the first human sin, for Adam and for every child of Adam after him.

sections.title: Common Misunderstandings on Both Sides
sections.body:
It is worth clearing away several frequent confusions, because both traditions are often misstated in debate.

First: Islam does not teach that human beings are sinless or naturally good in a naive sense. The Quran describes Adam's disobedience without excuse — 'And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred' (Quran 20:121) — and expects every person to sin and to repent throughout life. The disagreement with Christianity is not about whether we sin; it is about whether we inherit sin.

Second: original sin does not mean Christians believe babies have personally done something wrong. Catholic teaching is explicit that original sin is a state contracted by inheritance, not an act committed by the child. Fair comparison requires stating the doctrine as its adherents state it.

Third: not all Christians hold the Augustinian doctrine. Eastern Orthodox Christianity speaks of ancestral sin — inherited mortality and a weakened nature, but not inherited guilt. A Christian reader from that tradition will already agree with part of the Islamic critique of the Western formulation.

Fourth: the Quran's Adam is not a trivialized figure whose sin 'did not matter.' His sin mattered enough that the Quran records it candidly; what differs is the remedy — immediate, personal repentance met by immediate, personal forgiveness.

Fifth: pointing to Ezekiel 18 is not a claim that the Bible contradicts itself beyond repair. Christian theologians have harmonizations, some of them careful and serious. The comparative question a seeker should ask is more modest: which overall framework requires the fewer and less strained harmonizations to hold its scriptures together?

sections.title: The Islamic Conclusion
sections.body:
From the texts quoted above, the Islamic position assembles itself into a simple, coherent whole. God is just: 'no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another' (Quran 17:15), and He 'does not charge a soul except [with that within] its capacity' (Quran 2:286) — so no one can be born owing a debt for an act committed before their birth. God is merciful: when the first sin happened, He 'accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful' (Quran 2:37) — so no unpaid debt was left to accumulate. And God created us sound: every child is born upon 'the fitrah of Allah upon which He has created [all] people' (Quran 30:30).

On this view, salvation needs no inherited crisis and no third party to absorb guilt. Each person stands before God directly, falls directly, and returns directly, as Adam did. Muslims find this not only just and merciful but also in deep harmony with the Bible's own words in Ezekiel 18:20 and Deuteronomy 24:16, and with the innocence Jesus pointed to when he told his followers to 'become as little children' (Matthew 18:3). The invitation of this article is simply to read all of these texts slowly, side by side, and ask which picture of God — and of the newborn child — they most naturally describe.

sections.title: Frequently Asked Questions
sections.body:
Q: Does Islam deny that Adam and Eve sinned?
A: No. The Quran says plainly that 'Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred' (Quran 20:121). What Islam denies is that this sin, once repented of and forgiven (Quran 20:122, 2:37), was transmitted to Adam's descendants as guilt or as a corrupted nature requiring redemption.

Q: If there is no original sin, why does everyone still die?
A: In Islam, death is not a punishment inherited from Adam; it is part of how God designed this earthly life as a test, with the return to Him at its end (Quran 6:164 speaks of that return). Mortality is our created condition, not our sentence.

Q: What exactly is the fitrah?
A: The fitrah is the innate disposition upon which God creates every human being (Quran 30:30) — an inborn orientation toward its Creator and toward moral truth. Upbringing and environment can cover it over, but it is the factory setting, so to speak, of every soul.

Q: Do all Christians believe in original sin the same way?
A: No. The doctrine of inherited guilt is a Western development associated especially with Augustine. Eastern Orthodox Christianity teaches 'ancestral sin' instead: humanity inherits the consequences of the fall — mortality and weakness — but not Adam's guilt.

Q: What does Islam say about babies who die?
A: Since children are born pure and are not accountable before maturity, Islam has no concept of an unbaptized infant at risk. The mainstream position among Muslim scholars is that children who die rest in God's mercy.

sections.title: Sources and Further Reading
sections.body:
Every Quran verse in this article was retrieved from api.alquran.cloud (Tanzil Uthmani Arabic text with the Saheeh International translation) and every Bible verse from bible-api.com (World English Bible, a public-domain translation), and each is quoted verbatim as returned. The historical claims about Augustine of Hippo (354–430), the condemnation of Pelagianism in 418, and the councils of Carthage (418) and Orange (529) were checked against the Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy entries on Augustine and on sin in Christian thought.

For readers who want to go deeper, the further-reading list below includes classic Christian sources, so you can encounter the doctrine of original sin as its greatest exponents taught it; a standard academic survey of Christian theology; an Eastern Orthodox account of ancestral sin; and accessible treatments of the Islamic understanding of human nature, sin, and repentance. Reading both sides in their own words is the fairest path — and this library exists to make that path easier.

quranVerses.arabic: مَّنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَإِنَّمَا يَضِلُّ عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۗ وَمَا كُنَّا مُعَذِّبِينَ حَتَّىٰ نَبْعَثَ رَسُولًۭا
quranVerses.translation: Whoever is guided is only guided for [the benefit of] his soul. And whoever errs only errs against it. And no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. And never would We punish until We sent a messenger.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: قُلْ أَغَيْرَ ٱللَّهِ أَبْغِى رَبًّۭا وَهُوَ رَبُّ كُلِّ شَىْءٍۢ ۚ وَلَا تَكْسِبُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ إِلَّا عَلَيْهَا ۚ وَلَا تَزِرُ وَازِرَةٌۭ وِزْرَ أُخْرَىٰ ۚ ثُمَّ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكُم مَّرْجِعُكُمْ فَيُنَبِّئُكُم بِمَا كُنتُمْ فِيهِ تَخْتَلِفُونَ
quranVerses.translation: Say, "Is it other than Allah I should desire as a lord while He is the Lord of all things? And every soul earns not [blame] except against itself, and no bearer of burdens will bear the burden of another. Then to your Lord is your return, and He will inform you concerning that over which you used to differ."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: لَا يُكَلِّفُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسًا إِلَّا وُسْعَهَا ۚ لَهَا مَا كَسَبَتْ وَعَلَيْهَا مَا ٱكْتَسَبَتْ ۗ رَبَّنَا لَا تُؤَاخِذْنَآ إِن نَّسِينَآ أَوْ أَخْطَأْنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تَحْمِلْ عَلَيْنَآ إِصْرًۭا كَمَا حَمَلْتَهُۥ عَلَى ٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِنَا ۚ رَبَّنَا وَلَا تُحَمِّلْنَا مَا لَا طَاقَةَ لَنَا بِهِۦ ۖ وَٱعْفُ عَنَّا وَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا وَٱرْحَمْنَآ ۚ أَنتَ مَوْلَىٰنَا فَٱنصُرْنَا عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَٰفِرِينَ
quranVerses.translation: Allah does not charge a soul except [with that within] its capacity. It will have [the consequence of] what [good] it has gained, and it will bear [the consequence of] what [evil] it has earned. "Our Lord, do not impose blame upon us if we have forgotten or erred. Our Lord, and lay not upon us a burden like that which You laid upon those before us. Our Lord, and burden us not with that which we have no ability to bear. And pardon us; and forgive us; and have mercy upon us. You are our protector, so give us victory over the disbelieving people."
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَأَكَلَا مِنْهَا فَبَدَتْ لَهُمَا سَوْءَٰتُهُمَا وَطَفِقَا يَخْصِفَانِ عَلَيْهِمَا مِن وَرَقِ ٱلْجَنَّةِ ۚ وَعَصَىٰٓ ءَادَمُ رَبَّهُۥ فَغَوَىٰ
quranVerses.translation: And Adam and his wife ate of it, and their private parts became apparent to them, and they began to fasten over themselves from the leaves of Paradise. And Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: ثُمَّ ٱجْتَبَٰهُ رَبُّهُۥ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدَىٰ
quranVerses.translation: Then his Lord chose him and turned to him in forgiveness and guided [him].
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًۭا ۚ فِطْرَتَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّتِى فَطَرَ ٱلنَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ ٱلدِّينُ ٱلْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ ٱلنَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ
quranVerses.translation: So direct your face toward the religion, inclining to truth. [Adhere to] the fitrah of Allah upon which He has created [all] people. No change should there be in the creation of Allah. That is the correct religion, but most of the people do not know.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

quranVerses.arabic: فَتَلَقَّىٰٓ ءَادَمُ مِن رَّبِّهِۦ كَلِمَٰتٍۢ فَتَابَ عَلَيْهِ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ هُوَ ٱلتَّوَّابُ ٱلرَّحِيمُ
quranVerses.translation: Then Adam received from his Lord [some] words, and He accepted his repentance. Indeed, it is He who is the Accepting of repentance, the Merciful.
quranVerses.sourceAttribution: api.alquran.cloud — Tanzil Uthmani text; Saheeh International translation

bibleVerses.text: Therefore as sin entered into the world through one man, and death through sin; and so death passed to all men, because all sinned.

Behold, I was born in iniquity.
In sin my mother conceived me.

The soul who sins, he shall die. The son shall not bear the iniquity of the father, neither shall the father bear the iniquity of the son. The righteousness of the righteous shall be on him, and the wickedness of the wicked shall be on him.
The fathers shall not be put to death for the children, neither shall the children be put to death for the fathers. Every man shall be put to death for his own sin.

and said,
“Most certainly I tell you, unless you turn, and become as little children, you will in no way enter into the Kingdom of Heaven.

faq.question: Does Islam deny that Adam and Eve sinned?
faq.answer: No. The Quran says plainly that 'Adam disobeyed his Lord and erred' (Quran 20:121). What Islam denies is that this sin, once repented of and forgiven (Quran 20:122, 2:37), was transmitted to Adam's descendants as guilt or as a corrupted nature requiring redemption.

faq.question: If there is no original sin, why does everyone still die?
faq.answer: In Islam, death is not a punishment inherited from Adam; it is part of how God designed this earthly life as a test, with the return to Him at its end. Mortality is our created condition, not our sentence.

faq.question: What exactly is the fitrah?
faq.answer: The fitrah is the innate disposition upon which God creates every human being (Quran 30:30) — an inborn orientation toward its Creator and toward moral truth. Upbringing and environment can cover it over, but it is the natural starting state of every soul.

faq.question: Do all Christians believe in original sin the same way?
faq.answer: No. The doctrine of inherited guilt is a Western development associated especially with Augustine of Hippo. Eastern Orthodox Christianity teaches 'ancestral sin' instead: humanity inherits the consequences of the fall — mortality and weakness — but not Adam's guilt.

faq.question: What does Islam say about babies who die?
faq.answer: Since children are born pure and are not accountable before maturity, Islam has no concept of an unbaptized infant at risk. The mainstream position among Muslim scholars is that children who die rest in God's mercy.

furtherReading.title: Confessions
furtherReading.author: Augustine of Hippo
furtherReading.note: The classic spiritual autobiography in which Augustine's convictions about human weakness and divine grace — the soil of the original sin doctrine — take shape. Read it to meet the doctrine's greatest exponent in his own voice.

furtherReading.title: City of God
furtherReading.author: Augustine of Hippo
furtherReading.note: Augustine's mature theological masterwork, including his fullest account of the fall of Adam and its consequences for humanity.

furtherReading.title: Christian Theology: An Introduction
furtherReading.author: Alister E. McGrath
furtherReading.note: A widely used academic textbook that surveys the development of the doctrine of original sin, the Pelagian controversy, and the differences among Catholic, Protestant, and Orthodox positions.

furtherReading.title: Byzantine Theology: Historical Trends and Doctrinal Themes
furtherReading.author: John Meyendorff
furtherReading.note: A standard Eastern Orthodox account, useful for seeing how the Christian East framed ancestral sin without Augustine's doctrine of inherited guilt.

furtherReading.title: The Cambridge Companion to Classical Islamic Theology
furtherReading.author: Tim Winter (ed.)
furtherReading.note: Scholarly essays on Islamic theology, including human nature, moral responsibility, and sin in the classical Islamic tradition.

furtherReading.title: Islam in Focus
furtherReading.author: Hammudah Abdalati
furtherReading.note: An accessible introduction to Islamic beliefs and practice, including a beginner-friendly discussion of why Islam has no doctrine of original sin.
